narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid upper-middle-class family background, you've put in the effort to pursue law school, including scoring a respectable 36.50 on your LSAT and maintaining a 3.30 undergraduate GPA, which is actually a bit above average for applicants like you. You're planning to attend full-time, just like the vast majority of students in our dataset at 92.7%. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 52.7%—just over the tipping point that led to your application's rejection. This decision was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from earlier years in school: your Year 3 decile of 2 was the single biggest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, well below the typical 5.53 average and signaling weaker performance relative to peers. Your Year 1 decile of 3 also contributed notably in the same direction, again lower than the average of 5.71, making these two the most influential red flags for the model. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA stood out positively, pulling the prediction toward success more than any other favorable factor by being stronger than most applicants' 3.21 average. Your LSAT score, nearly spot-on with the 36.70 average, and your upper-middle family income also helped nudge things toward passing, though not enough to offset the deciles. Compared to typical students who get approved, your profile aligns well on GPA, LSAT, and background but falls short on those key school deciles, which the model sees as the strongest predictors of bar exam struggles. We understand this is disappointing, especially when you've shown strengths in other areas that many applicants lack. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, as improving those could dramatically shift future predictions in your favor. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources or admissions counseling to address these gaps head-on. Keep pushing—you have the foundation to succeed with some focused improvements. We're rooting for you.
